## The Success of Physics

Any attempt to rethink foundations should begin with respect.

Modern physics is not a broken enterprise waiting to be replaced. It is an extraordinary intellectual achievement: precise in prediction, deep in structure, and astonishingly successful in its ability to organize the behavior of the physical world. The mathematical frameworks developed across classical mechanics, relativity, quantum theory, and field theory have earned their authority.

That success matters here. It sets the burden for any alternative starting point. A new framework does not earn attention by sounding simpler, more intuitive, or more philosophically satisfying. It earns attention only if it can preserve the explanatory and predictive burden carried by the framework it seeks to reorganize.

At the same time, success does not answer every kind of question. A theory may work magnificently and still leave open the question of which of its concepts are truly primitive, which are derived, and which have become central more by historical organization than by deepest necessity. Practical indispensability and foundational priority are not always the same thing.

That is the opening space in which this book operates. It does not begin from the belief that standard physics has failed. It begins from the possibility that some of its most familiar structures may not be the most economical place to begin.
